questions.question: A university library management system needs to process and sort a large collection of book records based on multiple attributes (title, author, publication year). Explain how you would apply the Divide and Conquer strategy using Object Oriented Programming principles in Python to solve this problem efficiently. Include a Python class design with appropriate methods, discuss the algorithmic complexity, and justify why this approach is better than a brute-force method for large datasets.
keyPoints: Define Book class with relevant attributes and comparison methods
Implement merge sort as divide-and-conquer strategy with recursive decomposition
Show conquer phase by merging sorted sublists
Analyze O(n log n) time complexity vs O(n²) for brute force
Discuss how OOP principles (encapsulation, polymorphism) support the solution

questions.question: Consider a scenario where a delivery robot must find the shortest path in a grid-based warehouse from start to destination while avoiding obstacles. Explain how you would apply the Greedy strategy to solve this problem in Python. Provide a code snippet demonstrating the greedy choice property and explain why this approach may not always yield an optimal solution.
keyPoints: Implement greedy approach choosing locally optimal path at each step
Use heuristic function (e.g., Manhattan distance) for decision making
Show code with priority queue for greedy selection
Discuss when greedy works (simple terrain) vs fails (complex mazes)
Explain the Greedy Choice Property requirement for optimality

questions.question: An e-commerce platform needs to calculate the minimum cost to ship products from multiple warehouses to multiple retail stores, where each warehouse has limited supply and each store has specific demand. Design a Python application using Dynamic Programming to solve this optimization problem. Your answer should include: (a) Problem formulation with state definition, (b) Recurrence relation, (c) Complete Python code with OOP implementation, and (d) Analysis of time and space complexity.
keyPoints: Define Warehouse, Store, and ShippingOptimizer classes
State: DP[i][j] represents minimum cost to satisfy first i warehouses and j stores
Recurrence: DP[i][j] = min(DP[i-1][j-k] + cost[i][j]*k) for k in range(0, min(supply[i], demand[j])+1)
Implement bottom-up DP with tabulation
Analyze O(n*m*min(supply, demand)) time and O(n*m) space complexity

questions.question: A sudoku puzzle validator in Python needs to check if a 9x9 grid follows all game rules. Demonstrate how the Backtracking strategy can be applied using Object Oriented Programming. Show the class structure and the recursive backtracking method with appropriate pruning techniques to efficiently validate the grid.
KeyPoints: Design SudokuBoard class with validation methods
Implement backtracking with recursive try-fill approach
Use pruning: check row, column, and 3x3 box constraints before proceeding
Show isValid() method to verify placement feasibility
Discuss how backtracking reduces search space compared to brute force

questions.question: A placement cell needs to assign N student interns to N different companies based on preference rankings from both sides (students rank companies, companies rank students). This is a classic Stable Marriage Problem. Using Object Oriented Programming in Python, design a complete solution applying the Stable Marriage Algorithm with the following requirements: (a) Create classes for Student, Company, and PlacementManager, (b) Implement the Gale-Shapley algorithm showing how men (students) propose and women (companies) accept/reject, (c) Trace through a small example with 3 students and 3 companies, and (d) Prove that the algorithm terminates with a stable matching.
keyPoints: Define Student class with preference list and Company class with ranking dictionary
Implement PlacementManager to run Gale-Shapley algorithm
Show propose() and reject()/accept() methods simulating the algorithm

Trace: Student1 proposes to CompanyA → accepted, Student2 proposes to CompanyB → accepted, Student3 proposes to CompanyA → CompanyA prefers Student3 → rejects Student1, Student1 proposes to CompanyB → CompanyB rejects (prefers Student2), Student1 proposes to CompanyC → accepted
Prove termination: Each student proposes to each company at most once (N² proposals maximum), and companies can only accept better matches (no rejections cycle)
